headline: Data centres vs Johor's water: a moratorium that exempts the biggest consumers
context: Malaysia paused non-AI data centres after 11,000MW in applications overwhelmed the grid (effective demand ~7.5GW). The AI exemption, lobbied for by PIKOM and AMCHAM, protects hyperscalers and squeezes smaller local operators. Johor alone has RM182.96B approved.

cards.big: Malaysia is betting its future on data centres. Johor residents are asking who'll be left with water.
cards.sub: Gelang Patah residents protested a China-based site. 11,000MW in applications still dwarf the grid. Johor has RM182.96B approved.

cards.big: 11,000MW applied vs ~7.5GW real demand — and the moratorium exempts AI centres, the most power-hungry category.
cards.sub: AI racks consume 3–10× more power. PIKOM and AMCHAM lobbied for the carve-out. Sarawak diverted investment with surplus hydropower; Singapore reopened only with strict PUE rules.

cards.big: The moratorium cited water too — but no public data exists on actual water use per data centre in Johor.
cards.sub: Johor set a WUE threshold of 1.8 and planned four treatment plants worth RM1.41B. Orang Asli communities near proposed sites were absent from consultations.

cards.big: RM182.96B is approved in Johor alone — yet the pause shields hyperscalers while local operators face shutdown.
cards.sub: Microsoft, Google and ByteDance structured investments as AI-focused, bypassing the pause. Smaller Malaysian firms cannot relabel.

cards.big: The real filter is not sustainability — it is lobbying power. The ceiling has a hole sized for the biggest players.
Like installing a water meter in every house but exempting the swimming pools.
cards.sub: A moratorium with exemptions for the largest consumers signals sustainability as a constraint for the small, not the powerful.

cards.big: The ambition is not wrong — it is ungoverned. Residents should not have to protest to trigger what the numbers already demanded.
